name: Mitch Miller
abstract: Mitchell William Miller (July 4, 1911 – July 31, 2010) was an American choral conductor, record producer, record-industry executive, and professional oboist. He was involved in almost all aspects of the industry, particularly as a conductor and artists and repertoire (A&R) man.

works.t: Tristan und Isolde: Act III. Prelude
Andante for Solo Oboe and Small Orchestra
Sinfonia Concertante in B-flat Major, H.I:105

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Conniff
about_quotes.evidence: The first break I got was through Mitch Miller... Mitch was tickled to death. He thought this is great. This guy is great. So he started giving me all his artists.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Conniff interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmie Crawford
about_quotes.evidence: I worked a lot for Mitch Miller. Just name them all, they all were engineers, like, and directors for the date.

about_quotes.speaker: Barney Bigard
about_quotes.evidence: He had Mitch Miller. You remember Mitch Miller? ... Yeah, with Jelly, yeah.

about_quotes.speaker: David Albert Francis
about_quotes.evidence: I did the 'Colonel Bogie's March' with Mitch Miller, which was a big hit.

about_quotes.speaker: Al Gallodoro
about_quotes.evidence: Jack Federson, who's the president now, wrote to Mitch Miller, why don't you release it? ... You'd have to get a good manager, too, to open this thing up. See, what's terrible about it is that they have the masters and the records to be sold. ... You get enough kids to write to Mitch Miller out, but

about_quotes.speaker: Avakian George
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee mentions Mitch Miller in the context of professional interactions at Columbia Records (e.g., 'Martha Glaser approached him about recording Erroll', 'Martha had had a fight with Mitch', 'I didn't want any infringement with Mitch, big ego.
